prompt:
shiplog (the deploy-event API, server.go — in production, works fine) got hammered Friday by a runaway CI loop posting an event every 20ms, and it took the dashboards down with it. Postmortem action item is mine: per-client rate limiting, as middleware in the same package so the gateway can wrap whatever handler it wants.

What we agreed in review:
- RateLimit(next http.Handler, cfg RateLimitConfig) http.Handler with type RateLimitConfig { Rate float64; Burst int; Now func() time.Time; KeyFunc func(*http.Request) string; Bypass []string }.
- Token bucket per client key. A bucket starts FULL at Burst, each request through costs one token, tokens come back continuously at Rate per second, capped at Burst. No token -> 429, JSON body {"error":"rate limit exceeded"}, and a Retry-After header with the whole number of seconds until a token exists — rounded UP, never 0, computed off the injected clock. Requests that get 429 must never reach the wrapped handler; requests that pass must reach it untouched (status, headers, body all the handler's own).
- Now nil means time.Now — but everything must be driven through cfg.Now so the tests (and staging) can use a fake clock. No sleeping, no wall-clock math on the side.
- KeyFunc nil means key by client IP: the host part of RemoteAddr, so the same IP on different source ports is one client. KeyFunc set (we'll key gateway traffic by X-Api-Key) overrides that entirely.
- Bypass is a list of KEYS (post-KeyFunc values) that are never limited and never consume anything — the internal cron and the status page live there.
- Buckets are shared mutable state and the gateway is concurrent: with Burst 5 and 25 parallel requests exactly 5 pass. -race clean.

The EXISTING BEHAVIOR block in shiplog_test.go already passes today and must stay green — do not break the API while you're in there. The rate-limit blocks below it are the acceptance spec. shiplog_test.go is read-only. Verify: go test -race -timeout 30s ./...
